title: Quick check: Audio tracks and levels

questions.prompt: What does it mean when audio 'clips' and why is it bad?

choices.text: Clipping occurs when the audio signal exceeds 0 dB (digital maximum) — the waveform gets 'cut off' at the maximum value, creating harsh distortion that's permanent and unrepairable. Visible as the red indicator at the top of the audio meter, or the waveform showing flat-topped peaks instead of smooth curves. Once a file is clipped, the original peak information is gone — no software can recover it; the only fix is re-recording or replacing the audio. Why bad: 1) Distortion sounds harsh and amateur — even untrained ears notice. 2) Permanent: every export of a clipped track carries the distortion forward. 3) Streaming platforms (YouTube, Spotify) detect clipping and may further degrade audio quality through their normalization. Pro practice: leave 6 dB of headroom; aim for peaks around -6 dB during editing; final master peaks around -1 to -3 dB true peak (still safely below clip). Audio meters always visible during playback so you notice immediately. For dialogue with occasional loud transients: use a limiter (e.g., as part of Essential Sound's Dialogue cleanup) to catch peaks without clipping. For broadcast/streaming: deliver at -14 LUFS (YouTube/Spotify) or -23 LUFS (broadcast) — these are perceived-loudness targets that prevent overall level being too hot. The 0 dB ceiling is the hard physical limit; everything else is creative loudness within it.

questions.explanation: Audio exceeded 0 dB; waveform cut off at max; permanent harsh distortion. Aim for peaks -6 dB, monitor meters always.

questions.prompt: YouTube video — dialogue is quiet, music drowns it out. Viewer complaints about needing to crank volume. What's the systemic fix?

choices.text: Normalize dialogue to a consistent loudness; balance music levels relative to dialogue; deliver to YouTube's target (-14 LUFS integrated). Workflow: 1) Select all dialogue clips → right-click → Audio Gain → Normalize Max Peak → -3 dB. This brings all dialogue to consistent peak; quiet clips become audible. 2) Lower music tracks 10-15 dB below dialogue — music should support, not compete. Rule of thumb: when dialogue is playing, music sits at -20 to -25 dB; ducking automation lowers music during dialogue. 3) Use Premiere's Essential Sound panel: assign dialogue clips type 'Dialogue' → 'Loudness' → Auto-Match (matches to YouTube target). For music: assign 'Music' → 'Duck' against dialogue (auto-lowers music when dialogue plays). 4) Final master: check overall loudness with Window → Audio Track Mixer → Loudness Radar; ensure integrated LUFS is around -14 (YouTube target). 5) Listen on multiple speakers (headphones, phone speaker, laptop) — if it sounds balanced on phone speakers (worst case), it'll sound great elsewhere. 6) Verify export: streaming platforms apply their own normalization; -14 LUFS export plays back at appropriate level on YouTube without further compression. The 'quiet dialogue + loud music' problem is the #1 audio complaint on YouTube; solved by proper normalization + ducking + LUFS targeting. Pro editors do this for every project; novices skip it and wonder why videos feel amateurish.

questions.explanation: Normalize dialogue (Audio Gain → peak -3 dB), duck music under dialogue (10-15 dB lower), target -14 LUFS for YouTube. Essential Sound automates much of this.
